title: The Agentic Loop Lifecycle
content:
The 5-Step Client-Side Loop

The agentic loop is the canonical execution pattern for Claude tool use. It is a while loop keyed on stop_reason that forms the backbone of every agentic application. The five steps are:

  1. Send a request with your tools array and the user message.
  2. Claude responds with stop_reason: "tool_use" and one or more tool_use blocks.
  3. Execute each tool in your application code, collecting results.
  4. Send a new request containing the original messages, the assistant's response, and a user message with tool_result blocks.
  5. Repeat from step 2 while stop_reason is "tool_use".

The loop exits on any stop reason other than tool_use. This is the single decision point — stop_reason determines whether your loop continues or terminates.

All Eight stop_reason Values and Their Handlers

stop_reasonMeaningRequired Action
end_turnClaude has produced a final answerExit loop; present response to user
tool_useClaude wants to call one or more client toolsExecute tools, send tool_result, continue loop
max_tokensOutput hit the max_tokens limitExit loop; response may be truncated — retry with higher limit if needed
stop_sequenceA custom stop sequence was matchedExit loop; handle based on which sequence triggered
pause_turnServer-side loop hit iteration limit (server tools only)Re-send conversation including the paused response to let Claude continue
refusalClaude refused to respond (safety)Exit loop; log and handle gracefully — do NOT retry
model_context_window_exceededInput reached the model's context window limitExit loop; apply context management (compaction, clearing, or start fresh). Available by default on Sonnet 4.5+; earlier models need beta header model-context-window-exceeded-2025-08-26
compactionServer-side compaction summary generated (when pause_after_compaction: true)Append the compaction block to messages; optionally add content before continuing. Use to enforce token budgets by counting compaction events
Critical Exam Fact
The loop continues ONLY while stop_reason == "tool_use". Any other value — including pause_turn in the outer client loop — exits the standard tool-use loop. However, pause_turn requires a continuation request (a new API call passing the paused response back).

Three Tool Execution Categories

Anthropic organizes tools into three categories based on who defines the schema and who executes the code:

CategorySchema AuthorExecutionExamples
User-defined client toolsYouYour codeAny custom tool (get_weather, query_db)
Anthropic-schema client toolsAnthropicYour codebash_20250124, text_editor_20250728, computer_20251124, memory_20250818
Server-executed toolsAnthropicAnthropic's serversweb_search_20260209, web_fetch_20260209, code_execution_20260120

Anthropic-schema client tools are trained-in — Claude has been optimized on thousands of successful trajectories using these exact schemas, so it calls them more reliably than custom tools with similar functionality.

server_tool_use vs tool_use Blocks

The block type and ID prefix tell you exactly who must handle execution:

Block TypeID PrefixWho ExecutesYou Send tool_result?
tool_usetoolu_Your applicationYes — you must
server_tool_usesrvtoolu_Anthropic's serversNo — never

Server tool results appear immediately after the server_tool_use block in the same assistant turn. By the time you see them, execution is already complete. The server runs its own internal loop and may trigger several searches or executions before responding.

Handling pause_turn

When the server-side loop hits its iteration limit (default 10), the response returns with stop_reason: "pause_turn". To continue:

  • Pass the paused response back as-is in a subsequent request
  • You can optionally modify the content before continuing
  • Preserve tool state — include the same tools in the continuation request

The paused response may contain server_tool_use blocks without corresponding server_tool_result blocks — this is expected.

Loop Guards

In production, you must implement safeguards to prevent runaway loops:

  • Maximum iteration count: Cap the number of loop iterations (e.g., 25-50 turns)
  • Token budget: Track cumulative token usage and exit when approaching limits
  • Cost budget: Set a maximum dollar spend (the Agent SDK supports max_budget_usd)
  • Timeout: Set wall-clock time limits for the entire loop
// Agentic loop with guards
let iterations = 0;
const MAX_ITERATIONS = 30;

while (iterations < MAX_ITERATIONS) {
  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: "claude-sonnet-4-6-20250514",
    messages: conversationHistory,
    tools: toolDefinitions,
  });
  conversationHistory.push({ role: "assistant", content: response.content });

  if (response.stop_reason === "end_turn" || response.stop_reason === "refusal") {
    break; // Done
  }
  if (response.stop_reason === "tool_use") {
    const toolResults = [];
    for (const block of response.content) {
      if (block.type === "tool_use") {
        const result = await executeTool(block.name, block.input);
        toolResults.push({ type: "tool_result", tool_use_id: block.id, content: result });
      }
    }
    conversationHistory.push({ role: "user", content: toolResults });
  }
  if (response.stop_reason === "pause_turn") {
    // Server-side loop paused — re-send to continue
    conversationHistory.push({ role: "user", content: "Please continue." });
  }
  iterations++;
}
Exam Preparation Tips
  • Know all 8 stop_reason values and the correct handler for each
  • Remember: srvtoolu_ prefix = server tool, toolu_ prefix = client tool
  • Never construct a tool_result for server-executed tools
  • pause_turn is specific to server tools hitting their iteration limit
  • Always implement loop guards in production — unbounded loops are an anti-pattern
summary: The agentic loop is a while loop keyed on stop_reason that orchestrates Claude's tool use. It cycles through sending requests, checking stop_reason, executing tools, and feeding results back. There are 8 stop_reason values (end_turn, tool_use, max_tokens, stop_sequence, pause_turn, refusal, model_context_window_exceeded, compaction), 3 tool execution categories, and distinct block types (tool_use/server_tool_use) with different ID prefixes.

key_concepts.definition: Field in every API response indicating why Claude stopped generating — the single decision point in the agentic loop.
Indicates Claude wants to call client tools. The only stop_reason that continues the loop.
Server-side tool loop hit its iteration limit (default 10). Re-send conversation including the paused response to continue.
Block type for server-executed tools. Uses srvtoolu_ ID prefix. You never construct a tool_result for these.
Tools with Anthropic-published schemas (bash, text_editor, computer, memory) but client-side execution. Trained-in for better reliability.
Production safeguards (max iterations, token budget, cost limits, timeouts) to prevent runaway agentic loops.
Stop reason indicating Claude declined a request for safety reasons. Must be handled separately — do not retry.

Stop reason indicating the input reached the model's context window limit. Apply context management strategies (compaction, clearing) or start fresh. Available by default on Sonnet 4.5+; earlier models require beta header model-context-window-exceeded-2025-08-26.

Stop reason returned when pause_after_compaction is true and a compaction summary was generated. Allows adding content before continuing. Useful for enforcing token budgets by counting compaction events.

title: Tool Use Message Contract
content:
tool_use and tool_result Block Structure

Tool use in Claude follows a strict message contract. Claude requests tool execution via tool_use blocks in assistant messages, and your application returns results via tool_result blocks in user messages. These two block types are the entire interface.

tool_use Block (from Claude)

{
  "type": "tool_use",
  "id": "toolu_01A09q90qw90lq917835lq9",
  "name": "get_weather",
  "input": { "location": "San Francisco, CA", "unit": "celsius" }
}

The id field uses the toolu_ prefix for client tools and srvtoolu_ for server tools. The name must match a tool from your tools array. The input contains JSON arguments matching the tool's input_schema.

tool_result Block (from your code)

{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01A09q90qw90lq917835lq9",
  "content": "Current weather: 15°C, partly cloudy"
}
FieldRequiredDescription
tool_use_idYesThe id from the corresponding tool_use request
contentNoString, array of content blocks, or omitted for empty result
is_errorNoSet to true if tool execution resulted in an error

Critical Ordering Rules

These Cause 400 Errors

Violating ordering rules produces hard API errors, not degraded responses.

Rule 1: No intermediate messages. You CANNOT include any messages between the assistant's tool_use message and the user's tool_result message. The tool result must immediately follow the tool use in message history.

Rule 2: tool_result blocks come FIRST. In the user message containing tool results, all tool_result blocks must appear before any text content in the content array.

// WRONG — causes 400 error (text before tool_result):
{"role": "user", "content": [
  {"type": "text", "text": "Here are the results:"},
  {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01"}
]}

// CORRECT — tool_result first, then text:
{"role": "user", "content": [
  {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01", "content": "result"},
  {"type": "text", "text": "What should I do next?"}
]}

Content Formats

The content field in tool_result supports multiple formats:

  • Simple string: "content": "15 degrees"
  • Text block array: "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "15 degrees"}]
  • Document block: "content": [{"type": "document", "source": {"type": "text", "media_type": "text/plain", "data": "..."}}]
  • Image block: "content": [{"type": "image", "source": {"type": "base64", "media_type": "image/jpeg", "data": "..."}}]
  • Empty: Omit content entirely for void operations

Error Handling with is_error

When tool execution fails, return a tool_result with is_error: true and a descriptive error message. Claude will incorporate the error into its reasoning and may retry.

{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01A09q90qw90lq917835lq9",
  "content": "ConnectionError: weather service API unavailable (HTTP 500)",
  "is_error": true
}

Best practice: Write instructive error messages that explain what went wrong and what to try next. Claude retries invalid tool calls 2-3 times with corrections before apologizing.

Anti-Pattern: Swallowing Errors
Never silently drop a tool call or return an empty result when an error occurred. Always set is_error: true so Claude can reason about the failure and adapt its strategy.

Parallel Tool Use and Result Formatting

By default, Claude may request multiple tools in a single response. Multiple tool_use blocks appear in the assistant's content array. You must return all results in a single user message — not separate messages.

// WRONG — separate messages reduce parallel tool use:
[
  {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01", "content": "result1"}]},
  {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_02", "content": "result2"}]}
]

// CORRECT — single message with all results:
[
  {"role": "user", "content": [
    {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01", "content": "result1"},
    {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_02", "content": "result2"}
  ]}
]

disable_parallel_tool_use

You can limit tool calls per response with disable_parallel_tool_use:

SettingWith tool_choice: autoWith tool_choice: any/tool
disable_parallel_tool_use: false (default)Zero or more toolsOne or more tools
disable_parallel_tool_use: trueAt most one toolExactly one tool
Key Exam Facts
  • tool_result blocks must come FIRST in the content array — text after all results
  • No intermediate messages between tool_use and tool_result
  • Parallel results must go in a single user message, not separate messages
  • is_error: true lets Claude reason about failures; empty/missing results lose that signal
  • Claude retries invalid tool calls 2-3 times before giving up
  • disable_parallel_tool_use: true + auto = at most one; + any/tool = exactly one
summary: The tool use message contract defines the exact structure of tool_use and tool_result blocks, their ordering rules, content formats, and error handling. Critical rules include: tool_result must immediately follow tool_use, tool_result blocks must come first in the content array, and parallel results must be in a single user message.

key_concepts.definition: Assistant-side block containing tool name, unique id (toolu_ prefix), and JSON input parameters. Claude's request to execute a tool.
User-side block containing tool_use_id, content (string/array/empty), and optional is_error flag. Your response after executing the tool.
In a user message with tool results, all tool_result blocks must appear before any text blocks. Violating this causes a 400 error.
Boolean field on tool_result that tells Claude the execution failed. Claude incorporates the error and may retry 2-3 times.
When Claude requests multiple tools, all results must be returned in a single user message, not separate messages.
When true, limits Claude to at most one tool call per response (with auto) or exactly one (with any/tool).

title: Tool Categories and Configuration
content:
Three Tool Categories Deep Dive

Every tool in the Claude ecosystem falls into one of three categories. Understanding these categories is essential for choosing the right execution model and configuring tools correctly.

CategoryType Field ExamplesExecutionYou Handle
User-defined client toolsNo type field — just name/description/input_schemaClientSchema + execution + agentic loop
Anthropic-schema client toolsbash_20250124, text_editor_20250728, computer_20251124, memory_20250818ClientExecution + agentic loop (schema trained-in)
Server-executed toolsweb_search_20260209, web_fetch_20260209, code_execution_20260120ServerEnable tool, read final answer

Client Tool Definition Schema

{
  "name": "get_weather",
  "description": "Get current weather for a location. Returns temperature and conditions.",
  "input_schema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "location": {"type": "string", "description": "City and state, e.g. San Francisco, CA"},
      "unit": {"type": "string", "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"]}
    },
    "required": ["location"]
  }
}

Tool names must match ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$. The description is critical — Claude reads it to decide when to call the tool.

tool_choice: auto, any, tool, none

ValueBehaviorExtended Thinking Support
autoClaude decides whether to call any tools. Default when tools are provided.Yes
anyClaude MUST use one of the provided toolsNo
toolClaude MUST use a specific named tool: {"type": "tool", "name": "get_weather"}No
noneClaude cannot use any tools. Default when no tools provided.Yes

Prefill Behavior

When tool_choice is any or tool, the API prefills the assistant message to force tool use. This means the model will NOT emit natural language before tool_use blocks. This is important for workflows where you need guaranteed tool invocation without preamble text.

Extended Thinking Restrictions

Compatibility Constraint
Extended thinking only supports tool_choice: auto and tool_choice: none. Using any or tool with extended thinking will cause an error.

strict: true — Grammar-Constrained Sampling

The strict: true property on tool definitions uses grammar-constrained sampling to guarantee tool inputs match your JSON Schema exactly.

  • Guarantees: Tool input strictly follows input_schema; tool name is always valid
  • Caching: Compiled schemas are cached up to 24 hours since last use
  • PHI warning: Do NOT include PHI in tool schema definitions — schemas are cached separately from message content
  • Combine with tool_choice: any for guaranteed tool call AND schema compliance

defer_loading

Tools with defer_loading: true are excluded from the initial system prompt and discovered on demand via tool search. Key benefits:

  • Tools don't appear in the system-prompt prefix at all
  • Preserves prompt cache — adding deferred tools won't invalidate existing cache entries
  • Deferred tools are stripped before the cache key is computed

allowed_callers

ValueMeaning
"direct"Model can call this tool directly in a tool_use block (DEFAULT if omitted)
"code_execution_20260120"Code running inside a code execution sandbox can call this tool

Omitting "direct" means the tool is callable ONLY from within code execution — the model cannot call it directly.

Tool Token Overhead

Tool definitions consume tokens even before any tool is called. The system prompt overhead varies by model and tool_choice setting:

Modelsauto/none overheadany/tool overhead
Claude Opus 4.7, 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5346 tokens313 tokens
Claude Haiku 3.5264 tokens340 tokens

Each individual tool definition adds additional tokens proportional to its schema complexity. Monitor total tool token cost in applications with many tools — consider defer_loading or tool search to manage this.

Optional Tool Definition Properties

PropertyPurposeAvailable On
cache_controlSet a prompt-cache breakpoint at this tool definitionAll tools
strictGrammar-constrained schema validationAll except mcp_toolset
defer_loadingLoad on demand via tool searchAll tools
allowed_callersRestrict which callers can invoke the toolAll except mcp_toolset
input_examplesExample input objects to help ClaudeUser-defined and Anthropic-schema only (NOT server tools)
eager_input_streamingFine-grained input streamingUser-defined tools only
Key Exam Facts
  • Extended thinking only works with tool_choice: auto or none
  • any/tool prefills the assistant message — no natural language before tool calls
  • strict: true provides deterministic schema validation via grammar-constrained sampling
  • defer_loading: true preserves prompt cache when adding tools
  • input_examples are NOT supported for server-side tools
  • Tool name regex: ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$
summary: Tool categories (user-defined, Anthropic-schema, server-executed) determine who writes the schema and who executes the tool. Key configuration includes tool_choice (auto/any/tool/none with extended thinking restrictions), strict mode for grammar-constrained validation, defer_loading for prompt cache preservation, and allowed_callers for restricting tool invocation context.

key_concepts.definition: Controls whether Claude must use tools. auto (default with tools), any (must use a tool), tool (must use specific tool), none (no tools). Only auto and none support extended thinking.
Grammar-constrained sampling that guarantees tool inputs match JSON Schema. Compiled schemas cached 24 hours. Do not include PHI in schemas.
Excludes a tool from the initial system prompt; loaded on demand via tool search. Preserves prompt cache entries.
Restricts who can invoke a tool: 'direct' (model calls it) or 'code_execution_20260120' (only from sandbox). Omitting 'direct' blocks model access.
When tool_choice is any or tool, the API prefills the assistant message to force tool use. No natural language emitted before tool_use blocks.
Example input objects that help Claude use tools correctly. Only available on user-defined and Anthropic-schema tools, NOT server tools.

title: Workflow and Agent Patterns
content:
Workflows vs Agents

Anthropic draws a clear distinction between two architectural approaches:

ConceptDefinitionControl Model
WorkflowsLLMs and tools orchestrated through predefined code paths with predetermined sequencesYour code controls the flow
AgentsSystems where LLMs dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage, maintaining autonomous controlThe model controls the flow

Key guidance from Anthropic: "Start with simple LLM calls optimized with retrieval and contextual examples." Use workflows for predictability and consistency; use agents for flexibility and model-driven decision-making.

Prompt Chaining

Sequential LLM calls where each step processes the previous output. The key feature is programmatic "gates" that verify intermediate steps remain on track.

  • Structure: Step 1 output → gate check → Step 2 input → gate check → Step 3
  • Benefit: Trades latency for higher accuracy by simplifying individual tasks
  • Use cases: Marketing copy → translation; Document outline → validation → full document

Routing

Classifies input and directs to specialized downstream tasks. Each route can have its own optimized prompt.

  • Benefit: Separation of concerns enabling task-specific prompt optimization
  • Use cases: Customer service triage (general/refunds/technical); Model selection (simple → Haiku, complex → Sonnet)

Parallelization

Simultaneous LLM operations with programmatic output aggregation. Has two distinct variations:

VariationHow It WorksUse Case
SectioningIndependent subtasks run in parallelCode vulnerability reviews across multiple files
VotingSame task executed multiple times for diverse outputsContent appropriateness with configurable vote thresholds

Orchestrator-Workers (Hub-and-Spoke)

A central LLM dynamically decomposes tasks and delegates to worker LLMs. This is critically different from parallelization:

Key Distinction
In parallelization, subtasks are predefined in your code. In orchestrator-workers, the orchestrator dynamically determines the decomposition based on specific input analysis.

Use cases: Multi-file code modifications; multi-source research and analysis.

Evaluator-Optimizer

An iterative loop where one LLM generates and another evaluates with feedback. Success indicators include:

  • Clear evaluation criteria exist
  • Human feedback demonstrably improves outputs
  • LLM can articulate useful critiques

Use cases: Literary translation through iterative refinement; complex search requiring multiple analysis rounds.

Autonomous Agents

LLMs maintaining control over task execution through environmental feedback loops. The model plans independently after receiving initial direction. Key requirements:

  • Gain ground truth from the environment at each step (tool results, code execution feedback)
  • Pause capability at checkpoints or when encountering blockers
  • Stopping conditions (maximum iterations) to maintain operational control

As Anthropic puts it: "Agents are typically just LLMs using tools based on environmental feedback in a loop."

Three Principles for Agent Success

PrincipleDescription
SimplicityMinimize agent design complexity — fewer moving parts
TransparencyExplicitly display planning steps so users can verify
Agent-Computer Interface (ACI)Thoroughly document and test tools; apply poka-yoke principles to prevent errors

ACI is the agent analog of UX. During SWE-bench, Anthropic "spent more time optimizing tools than the overall prompt." Example: requiring absolute filepaths eliminated relative path errors.

When NOT to Use Agents

  • When the model can answer from training alone (no side effects needed)
  • For one-shot Q&A with no tool requirements
  • When tool-calling latency would dominate a trivial response
  • When a simple workflow pattern provides sufficient accuracy

Anthropic's heuristic: "If you're writing a regex to extract a decision from model output, that decision should have been a tool call."

Framework Guidance

Frameworks simplify initial implementation but create abstraction layers obscuring prompts and responses. Anthropic recommends starting with direct LLM API calls and using frameworks only when you understand the underlying code mechanisms.

Exam Preparation
  • Know the difference between workflows (predefined paths) and agents (dynamic control)
  • Understand all 5 workflow patterns and when to use each
  • Orchestrator-workers uses dynamic decomposition; parallelization uses predefined subtasks
  • The three principles: Simplicity, Transparency, ACI
  • Know the poka-yoke concept: designing tools to make errors harder
summary: Anthropic defines workflows as predefined code paths and agents as dynamically model-directed systems. The five core workflow patterns are prompt chaining, routing, parallelization (sectioning/voting), orchestrator-workers, and evaluator-optimizer. Three design principles — simplicity, transparency, and ACI — guide effective agent development.

key_concepts.definition: Workflows use predefined code paths with predetermined sequences. Agents dynamically direct their own processes and tool usage with autonomous control.
Sequential LLM calls with programmatic gates verifying intermediate steps. Trades latency for accuracy.
Classifies input and directs to specialized downstream tasks. Enables separation of concerns.
Simultaneous LLM operations with two variations: Sectioning (independent subtasks) and Voting (same task multiple times).
Central LLM dynamically decomposes tasks and delegates to workers. Key difference from parallelization: decomposition is dynamic, not predefined.
The principle of thoroughly documenting and testing tools, applying poka-yoke principles to make errors harder. The agent analog of UX design.
Error-proofing principle applied to tool design. Example: requiring absolute filepaths eliminated relative path errors in SWE-bench.

title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
content:
Coordinator-Subagent Pattern

In Claude Code's multi-agent architecture, the main conversation acts as the coordinator and spawns specialized subagents via the Agent tool (formerly called the Task tool, renamed in v2.1.63). Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions.

The core workflow:

  1. Claude encounters a task matching a subagent's description field
  2. Claude writes a task prompt based on the user's request
  3. A fresh, separate context window is created for the subagent
  4. The subagent works independently (reads files, runs commands, etc.)
  5. Only the subagent's final text response comes back to the main context, plus metadata with token counts and duration

Context Isolation

Context isolation is the defining feature of subagents. The subagent receives:

  • Its own system prompt (from the markdown body) — NOT the full Claude Code system prompt
  • Basic environment details (working directory, platform, shell, OS)
  • CLAUDE.md files (counted against subagent's context, not the parent's)

The subagent does NOT receive:

  • The parent's conversation history or tool results
  • The parent's auto memory (MEMORY.md) — unless the subagent has its own memory scope
  • The parent's system prompt

This isolation provides significant context savings. For example, a subagent might read 6,100 tokens of files in its own context but return only a 420-token summary to the parent.

Agent Teams (Experimental)

Agent teams are a separate, experimental feature (enabled with CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1). They differ fundamentally from subagents:

AspectSubagentsAgent Teams
CommunicationReport results back to parent onlyTeammates message each other directly
CoordinationParent manages all workShared task list with self-coordination
ContextOwn context, summary returnsFully independent sessions
Token costLower (results summarized)Higher (each is separate Claude instance)
NestingCannot spawn other subagentsCannot spawn their own teams

Managed Agents

Managed agents are deployed by organization admins via managed settings. They take the highest precedence in the scope priority hierarchy, overriding project and user subagents with the same name.

One-Level Delegation Constraint

Technical Limitation
Subagents cannot spawn other subagents. This is a hard architectural constraint, not a configuration option. If your workflow requires nested delegation, you must either use Skills (which run in the caller's context) or chain subagents sequentially from the main conversation.

This constraint exists to prevent infinite nesting and uncontrolled context multiplication. The main conversation remains the single orchestration point.

Summary Return Size

Subagents return only their final text response to the parent. According to the context engineering guide, summaries typically range from 1,000-2,000 tokens. All intermediate work — file reads, tool outputs, reasoning — stays in the subagent's context and is never seen by the parent.

Chaining vs Parallel Subagents

PatternHow It WorksBest For
ChainingInvoke subagents in sequence; pass context from one to nextDependent tasks where step N needs step N-1's output
ParallelInvoke multiple subagents concurrently; synthesize findingsIndependent research paths that don't share state
# Chaining example (natural language)
"Use the code-reviewer subagent to find performance issues,
 then use the optimizer subagent to fix them"

# Parallel example
"Research the authentication, database, and API modules
 in parallel using separate subagents"
Context Budget Warning
Running many parallel subagents that each return detailed results can consume significant main context. For sustained parallelism or when exceeding the context window, use agent teams instead of subagents.

Foreground vs Background Subagents

AspectForegroundBackground
BlockingBlocks main conversation until completeRuns concurrently
PermissionsPrompts passed through to userPre-approved before launch; auto-deny anything not pre-approved
Clarifying questionsPassed through to userTool call fails but subagent continues
TriggerDefault behaviorAsk "run in background" or press Ctrl+B
Key Exam Facts
  • Subagents cannot spawn other subagents — one level only
  • Context isolation: subagent gets its own context, returns only the summary
  • Agent Teams are experimental and require CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1
  • Task tool was renamed to Agent in v2.1.63; Task(...) still works as alias
  • Background subagents: permissions pre-approved, auto-deny anything not approved
  • Summary return is typically 1,000-2,000 tokens
summary: Multi-agent orchestration in Claude Code uses the coordinator-subagent pattern where the main conversation spawns specialized subagents via the Agent tool. Subagents run in isolated context windows and return only summaries. A hard constraint prevents subagents from spawning other subagents. Agent Teams are a separate experimental feature for peer-to-peer coordination.

key_concepts.definition: Main conversation acts as coordinator, spawning specialized subagents via the Agent tool. Each subagent has its own context window and returns only a summary.
Subagents receive their own system prompt and basic environment details, but NOT the parent's conversation history, tool results, or system prompt.
Subagents cannot spawn other subagents. This is a hard architectural limitation to prevent infinite nesting.
Experimental feature where multiple agents work in parallel with peer-to-peer communication via shared task lists and messaging. Requires CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1.
Foreground blocks the main conversation and passes permission prompts through. Background runs concurrently with pre-approved permissions.
Subagents typically return 1,000-2,000 token summaries to the parent. All intermediate work stays in the subagent's context.

title: Subagent Definition and Configuration
content:
Markdown + YAML Frontmatter Format

Subagents are defined as Markdown files with YAML frontmatter. The frontmatter contains configuration metadata, and the markdown body becomes the subagent's system prompt.

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
tools: Read, Glob, Grep
model: sonnet
---

You are a code reviewer. When invoked, analyze the code and provide
specific, actionable feedback on quality, security, and best practices.

The body becomes the system prompt. Subagents receive ONLY this system prompt (plus basic environment details), NOT the full Claude Code system prompt.

All 16 Subagent Frontmatter Fields

FieldRequiredDescription
nameYesUnique identifier using lowercase letters and hyphens
descriptionYesWhen Claude should delegate to this subagent — Claude reads this to decide
toolsNoComma-separated tool list. Inherits ALL tools if omitted (including MCP)
disallowedToolsNoTools to deny, removed from inherited or specified list
modelNosonnet, opus, haiku, full model ID, or inherit (default: inherit)
permissionModeNodefault, acceptEdits, auto, dontAsk, bypassPermissions, plan
maxTurnsNoMaximum agentic turns before the subagent stops
skillsNoSkills preloaded into context at startup (full content injected, not just available)
mcpServersNoMCP servers: string references or inline definitions
hooksNoLifecycle hooks scoped to this subagent
memoryNoPersistent memory scope: user, project, or local
backgroundNotrue to always run as background task (default: false)
effortNolow, medium, high, xhigh, max
isolationNoworktree for isolated git worktree copy; auto-cleaned if no changes
colorNoDisplay color: red, blue, green, yellow, purple, orange, pink, cyan
initialPromptNoAuto-submitted as first user turn when running as main session agent (via --agent)

Only name and description are required. All other fields have sensible defaults.

Built-in Subagents

AgentModelToolsPurpose
ExploreHaiku (fast)Read-only (Write/Edit denied)File discovery, code search, codebase exploration
PlanInherits from mainRead-only (Write/Edit denied)Codebase research for planning
General-purposeInherits from mainAll toolsComplex research, multi-step operations, code modifications

Explore supports thoroughness levels: quick (targeted lookups), medium (balanced), very thorough (comprehensive).

Scope Priority

When multiple subagents share the same name, the higher-priority location wins:

PriorityLocationScope
1 (highest)Managed settingsOrganization-wide
2--agents CLI flagCurrent session
3.claude/agents/Current project
4~/.claude/agents/All your projects
5 (lowest)Plugin's agents/ directoryWhere plugin enabled

Model Resolution Order

When Claude invokes a subagent, the model is resolved in this order:

  1. CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL environment variable (if set)
  2. Per-invocation model parameter (Claude can pass at invocation time)
  3. Subagent definition's model frontmatter
  4. Main conversation's model

Tool Restrictions

Tool access is controlled through three mechanisms:

  • tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash — Allowlist (only these tools)
  • disallowedTools: Write, Edit — Denylist (inherit everything except these)
  • If both set: disallowedTools applied first, then tools resolved against remaining

The Agent() syntax in tools controls subagent spawning (only applies to agents running as main thread with --agent):

  • Agent(worker, researcher) — Allow spawning only these subagent types
  • Agent without parentheses — Allow spawning any subagent
  • Agent omitted entirely — Cannot spawn any subagents

Permission Inheritance

Critical Inheritance Rules
  • If parent uses bypassPermissions or acceptEdits: takes precedence, cannot be overridden
  • If parent uses auto mode: subagent inherits auto mode, frontmatter permissionMode is ignored
  • Background subagents: permissions pre-approved before launch; auto-deny anything not pre-approved

Memory Scopes

ScopeLocationUse When
user~/.claude/agent-memory/<name>/Learnings across all projects
project.claude/agent-memory/<name>/Project-specific, shareable via VCS
local.claude/agent-memory-local/<name>/Project-specific, NOT checked in

When memory is enabled, the first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md is loaded at startup. Read, Write, and Edit tools are automatically enabled for memory management.

Worktree Isolation

Set isolation: worktree to run the subagent in a temporary git worktree. This gives the subagent an isolated copy of the repository, useful for parallel work without file conflicts. The worktree is automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes.

Plugin Security

Plugin subagents do NOT support hooks, mcpServers, or permissionMode frontmatter fields — they are silently ignored. To use these features, copy the agent file into .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/.

Key Exam Facts
  • Only name and description are required — all other 14 fields are optional
  • There are 16 total frontmatter fields
  • Omitting tools means inherit ALL tools (including MCP)
  • Explore uses Haiku; Plan and general-purpose inherit the main model
  • Scope priority: Managed > CLI > Project > User > Plugin
  • Model resolution: env var > invocation > frontmatter > main model
  • Plugin subagents silently ignore hooks, mcpServers, and permissionMode
  • Memory loads first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md
summary: Subagents are defined as Markdown files with YAML frontmatter containing 16 possible fields (only name and description required). Configuration covers tool restrictions, model resolution, permission inheritance, memory scopes, and worktree isolation. Built-in subagents include Explore (Haiku), Plan, and general-purpose.

key_concepts.definition: YAML frontmatter in Markdown files defining subagent configuration. 16 fields total, only name and description are required.
If tools field is omitted, the subagent inherits ALL tools including MCP. Use disallowedTools for denylist or tools for allowlist.
When multiple subagents share a name: Managed (1) > CLI flag (2) > .claude/agents/ (3) > ~/.claude/agents/ (4) > Plugin (5).
CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL env var > per-invocation parameter > frontmatter model > main conversation model.
If parent uses bypassPermissions, acceptEdits, or auto: these take precedence and cannot be overridden by subagent frontmatter.
Plugin subagents silently ignore hooks, mcpServers, and permissionMode fields. Copy to .claude/agents/ to use these features.
isolation: worktree creates a temporary git worktree copy for the subagent. Auto-cleaned if no changes made.

title: Hooks: Deterministic Enforcement
content:
26 Hook Event Types

Hooks are deterministic enforcement points that run outside the model's control. They provide programmatic control over Claude's behavior that cannot be bypassed by prompt injection. Claude Code supports 26 hook event types organized into categories:

Session Lifecycle (3)

EventCan Block?Description
SessionStartNoNew session or resume
SessionEndNoSession terminates
InstructionsLoadedNo (exit code ignored)CLAUDE.md or rules loaded

Per-Turn Events (3)

EventCan Block?Description
UserPromptSubmitYes (exit 2)User submits prompt
StopYes (exit 2)Claude finishes responding
StopFailureNoTurn ends due to API error

Tool Execution Loop (5)

EventCan Block?Description
PreToolUseYesBefore tool execution — the primary enforcement point
PostToolUseNo (tool already ran)After successful tool execution
PostToolUseFailureNoAfter tool execution fails
PermissionRequestYes (exit 2)Permission dialog appears
PermissionDeniedNo (use retry)Auto mode classifier denies call

Other Categories

Additional event categories include: Subagent (SubagentStart, SubagentStop), Task Management (TaskCreated, TaskCompleted), File & Environment (FileChanged, CwdChanged, ConfigChange), Context Management (PreCompact, PostCompact), Team & Notifications (TeammateIdle, Notification), Elicitation (Elicitation, ElicitationResult), and Worktree (WorktreeCreate, WorktreeRemove).

Four Handler Types

TypeDescriptionDefault Timeout
commandShell script or command — receives JSON via stdin600 seconds
httpHTTP POST to an endpoint30 seconds
promptLLM evaluates a prompt and returns decision30 seconds
agentSpawns a verifier agent60 seconds
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Bash",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "/absolute/path/to/validate.sh",
            "timeout": 30
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

Exit Codes: 0, 1, 2

CRITICAL: Exit Code 1 Does NOT Block

This is one of the most commonly confused facts. Exit code 1 = non-blocking error (execution continues). Exit code 2 = blocking error (enforces policy). Exit code 0 = success (allows the operation).

Exit CodeMeaningJSON Processing
0Success — operation allowedParses stdout JSON, added to debug log
1Non-blocking error — continues executionContinues (does NOT block)
2Blocking error — enforces policyIgnores stdout JSON, uses stderr as error message

PreToolUse Decision Control

PreToolUse hooks can return JSON with a permissionDecision field. The priority order is:

deny > defer > ask > allow

If any hook returns deny, the operation is blocked regardless of what other hooks return.

DecisionEffect
allowSkip permission prompt, allow tool execution
denyPrevent tool call — highest priority
askPrompt user to confirm
deferExit gracefully for resumption (requires -p flag, Agent SDK only)

updatedInput

PreToolUse hooks can modify tool parameters before execution using updatedInput:

{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
    "permissionDecision": "allow",
    "updatedInput": { "command": "modified-command --safe-flag" },
    "additionalContext": "Added safety flag per policy"
  }
}

Matcher Patterns

Matchers determine which tools trigger a hook:

PatternEvaluationExample
*, "", or omittedMatch allFires on every tool call
Only [a-zA-Z0-9_|]Exact string or pipe-separated listBash, Edit|Write
Contains other charactersJavaScript regex^Notebook, mcp__memory__.*

MCP Tool Naming

MCP tools follow the naming pattern mcp__<server>__<tool>. To match all tools from a server in a hook matcher, use: mcp__memory__.* (the .* is required since it triggers regex evaluation).

Config Locations

LocationScopeShareable
~/.claude/settings.jsonAll projectsNo (local machine)
.claude/settings.jsonSingle projectYes (commit to repo)
.claude/settings.local.jsonSingle projectNo (gitignored)
Managed policy settingsOrganization-wideYes (admin-controlled)

User/project settings cannot disable managed hooks.

Environment Variables

Hooks receive several environment variables: CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR (always), CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT and CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA (plugins only), CLAUDE_CODE_REMOTE (remote web environments), and CLAUDE_ENV_FILE (SessionStart, CwdChanged, FileChanged only).

Async Hooks

  • async: true — Runs in background, does NOT block
  • asyncRewake: true — Runs in background; Claude wakes on exit code 2 (stderr shown as system reminder)

Hook output injected into context is capped at 10,000 characters.

#!/bin/bash
# Example: Block reading .env files
input=$(cat)
file_path=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path')
if [[ "$file_path" == *".env"* ]]; then
  echo "Blocked: Cannot read .env files" >&2
  exit 2
fi
exit 0
Key Exam Facts
  • Exit code 1 = non-blocking (continues); exit code 2 = blocking (enforces)
  • permissionDecision priority: deny > defer > ask > allow
  • 26 total hook event types across 10 categories
  • 4 handler types: command, http, prompt, agent
  • MCP tool naming: mcp__<server>__<tool>
  • Hook output capped at 10,000 characters
  • Use absolute paths for hook scripts (security best practice)
  • Managed hooks cannot be disabled by user/project settings
summary: Hooks provide 26 deterministic enforcement points across Claude Code's lifecycle. They support 4 handler types (command, http, prompt, agent) and use exit codes 0/1/2 where critically exit code 1 does NOT block but exit code 2 does. PreToolUse hooks support permissionDecision (deny>defer>ask>allow) and updatedInput for parameter modification.

key_concepts.definition: 0 = success (allow), 1 = non-blocking error (continues), 2 = blocking error (enforces policy). Exit code 1 NOT blocking is a critical exam fact.
deny > defer > ask > allow. If any hook returns deny, the operation is blocked regardless of other hooks.
PreToolUse hooks can modify tool parameters before execution. Requires permissionDecision: allow and must be inside hookSpecificOutput.
Alphanumeric-only = exact match or pipe-separated list. Contains other chars = JavaScript regex. Omitted = match all.
Pattern: mcp__<server>__<tool>. Match all from server: mcp__memory__.* (requires .* for regex).
async: true runs in background without blocking. asyncRewake: true runs in background but wakes Claude on exit code 2.
Output injected into context is capped at 10,000 characters. Exceeding saves to file with preview and path.

title: Deterministic vs Probabilistic Control
content:
Three Layers of Control

Claude applications have three distinct enforcement layers, each with different guarantee levels. Understanding when to use each layer is essential for building robust, secure agentic systems.

Hooks = 100% Deterministic

Hooks provide deterministic, programmatic enforcement that runs outside the model's control. No amount of prompt injection can bypass a hook:

  • Exit code 2 from a PreToolUse hook will always block the tool call
  • permissionDecision: "deny" will always prevent execution
  • updatedInput will always modify tool parameters before execution
  • The model cannot skip, modify, or influence hooks — they run in your application process

Use cases for deterministic enforcement:

  • Blocking dangerous commands (rm -rf /)
  • Enforcing read-only access for specific agents
  • Validating SQL queries (only SELECT allowed)
  • Modifying commands before execution (adding safety flags)
  • Enforcing organization security policies via managed settings
#!/bin/bash
# Hook: Enforce read-only SQL queries
input=$(cat)
command=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.command')
if echo "$command" | grep -iqE '(INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|DROP|ALTER|CREATE|TRUNCATE)'; then
  echo "Blocked: Only SELECT queries are allowed" >&2
  exit 2
fi
exit 0

Prompts = Probabilistic

System prompts and tool descriptions provide probabilistic guidance. The model "usually" follows instructions but with no guarantee:

  • The model can deviate from instructions
  • Prompt injection attacks can potentially override guidance
  • Complex or ambiguous instructions may be misinterpreted
  • Best-effort compliance — not enforced by code

Use cases where prompts suffice:

  • Stylistic preferences (output format, tone)
  • General behavioral guidance (be concise, be thorough)
  • Tool selection hints (descriptions help Claude choose the right tool)
  • When the consequence of deviation is low

strict: true = API-Level Enforcement

Strict tool use provides a third category of enforcement — deterministic schema validation at the sampling level:

  • Uses grammar-constrained sampling to guarantee tool inputs match JSON Schema
  • Prevents type mismatches, missing required fields, invalid enum values
  • Operates at the sampling level — the model physically cannot generate non-compliant inputs
  • Complementary to hooks: strict validates schema (structure), hooks validate semantics (meaning)

Comparison Table

AspectHooks (Deterministic)Prompts (Probabilistic)strict: true (API-Level)
Guarantee level100% — code enforcedBest effort — model may deviate100% — grammar constrained
Bypass riskNone (runs outside model)Prompt injection possibleNone (sampling constraint)
LatencyAdds hook execution timeZero additional latencyMinimal (schema compilation)
FlexibilityBinary (allow/deny/modify)Nuanced reasoning possibleSchema validation only
ConfigurationJSON in settings filesNatural languageJSON Schema on tool definition
ScopeSpecific tool callsGeneral behaviorTool input structure
What it validatesSemantics (meaning)Behavior (intent)Structure (schema)

Use Cases for Each Layer

When to use hooks (deterministic):

  • Security-critical operations (file deletion, database writes, deployments)
  • Compliance requirements (data access controls, audit logging)
  • Operations that cannot be undone if done incorrectly
  • Organization-wide policies that must be enforced uniformly

When prompts are sufficient:

  • Output formatting preferences
  • Communication style and tone
  • General task prioritization guidance
  • Situations where occasional deviation is acceptable

When to use strict: true:

  • Tool inputs must conform to a specific schema
  • Preventing type errors in structured data
  • Guaranteeing required fields are always present
  • Combining with tool_choice: any for guaranteed call + schema compliance

Defense in Depth

The strongest agentic systems combine all three layers:

  1. Prompts guide Claude toward correct behavior (first line — shapes intent)
  2. strict: true ensures tool inputs are structurally valid (second line — validates format)
  3. Hooks enforce hard security boundaries (final line — blocks violations)
// Defense in depth example:
// Layer 1: System prompt says "Only query the users table"
// Layer 2: strict: true ensures the query parameter is a valid string
// Layer 3: PreToolUse hook validates the query contains only SELECT
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [{
      "matcher": "run_sql",
      "hooks": [{
        "type": "command",
        "command": "/scripts/validate-sql.sh"
      }]
    }]
  }
}
Design Principle

Prompts shape behavior. strict: true validates structure. Hooks enforce policy. Use prompts for the common case, strict for schema guarantees, and hooks for security boundaries. Never rely solely on prompts for security-critical operations.

Key Exam Facts
  • Hooks = 100% deterministic, runs outside model, cannot be bypassed by prompt injection
  • Prompts = probabilistic, best-effort, can be overridden
  • strict: true = deterministic schema validation at sampling level
  • Hooks validate semantics; strict validates structure; prompts guide behavior
  • Defense in depth: combine all three layers for robust security
  • Managed hooks cannot be disabled by user/project settings
summary: Claude applications have three enforcement layers: hooks (100% deterministic, runs outside model), prompts (probabilistic, best-effort), and strict: true (deterministic schema validation at sampling level). Defense in depth combines all three: prompts shape behavior, strict validates structure, hooks enforce security boundaries.

key_concepts.definition: Hooks run outside the model's control and provide 100% guaranteed enforcement. Cannot be bypassed by prompt injection. Use for security-critical operations.
System prompts and tool descriptions guide behavior but with no guarantee. The model may deviate. Use for low-consequence preferences.
Grammar-constrained sampling guarantees tool inputs match JSON Schema at the sampling level. Prevents structural violations. Complementary to hooks.
Combining all three layers: prompts guide intent, strict validates structure, hooks enforce security boundaries. The strongest approach for production systems.
Hooks validate semantics (is this query safe?). strict: true validates structure (does this match the schema?). Different concerns requiring different tools.
Organization-wide managed hooks cannot be disabled by user or project settings. This ensures uniform policy enforcement across an organization.

title: Managed Agents: Server-Side Agent Infrastructure
content:
What Are Managed Agents?

Managed Agents are a pre-built, configurable agent harness that runs in Anthropic's managed infrastructure. Instead of building your own agent loop, tool execution, and runtime, you get a fully managed environment with built-in prompt caching, compaction, and performance optimizations. They are best suited for long-running tasks and asynchronous work.

ApproachWhat It IsBest For
Messages APIDirect model prompting accessCustom agent loops and fine-grained control
Claude Managed AgentsPre-built, configurable agent harnessLong-running tasks and asynchronous work

The Four Pillars

Managed Agents are organized around four core concepts:

ConceptDescription
AgentThe model, system prompt, tools, MCP servers, and skills
EnvironmentA configured container template (packages, network access)
SessionA running agent instance within an environment, performing a specific task and generating outputs
EventsMessages exchanged between your application and the agent (user turns, tool results, status updates)
Critical Exam Fact
The four pillars are Agent, Environment, Session, and Events. A session starts idle and does NOT begin work until you send a user.message event.

How It Works (5 Steps)

  1. Create an agent — Define model, system prompt, tools, MCP servers, skills. Create once, reference by ID.
  2. Create an environment — Configure cloud container with packages, network access rules, mounted files.
  3. Start a session — Launch session referencing agent and environment IDs.
  4. Send events and stream responses — User messages as events; Claude streams back via SSE. Event history persisted server-side.
  5. Steer or interrupt — Send additional user events mid-execution, or interrupt to change direction.

Beta Access and Rate Limits

Beta header required: managed-agents-2026-04-01 (SDK sets automatically). Enabled by default for all API accounts.

OperationLimit
Create endpoints (agents, sessions, environments)60 requests per minute
Read endpoints (retrieve, list, stream)600 requests per minute

The agent_toolset_20260401

The built-in agent toolset provides a full set of pre-built tools:

ToolNameDescription
BashbashExecute bash commands in a shell session
ReadreadRead a file from the local filesystem
WritewriteWrite a file to the local filesystem
EditeditPerform string replacement in a file
GlobglobFast file pattern matching
GrepgrepText search using regex patterns
Web fetchweb_fetchFetch content from a URL
Web searchweb_searchSearch the web for information

Configuring the Toolset

Enable full toolset: {"type": "agent_toolset_20260401"}. Disable specific tools by setting configs array with {"name": "web_fetch", "enabled": false}. Enable ONLY specific tools by setting default_config.enabled to false, then enabling individual tools.

{
  "type": "agent_toolset_20260401",
  "default_config": { "enabled": false },
  "configs": [
    { "name": "bash", "enabled": true },
    { "name": "read", "enabled": true }
  ]
}

Cloud Environments

Environments provide configured containers for agent execution. Configuration type is cloud with networking type unrestricted by default. The environment specifies packages, network access rules, and mounted files. Environments are created once and referenced by ID when starting sessions.

Session Lifecycle

StatusDescription
idleWaiting for input (sessions start here)
runningAgent is actively executing
reschedulingTransient error, retrying automatically
terminatedUnrecoverable error

Creating a session provisions the environment but does NOT start work. You must send a user.message event to begin. The session acts as a state machine driven by events.

Session Streaming via SSE

Responses stream back in real-time via Server-Sent Events (SSE). Key event types:

  • agent.message — text content from Claude
  • agent.tool_use — tool call (includes tool name)
  • session.status_idle — agent finished work

Session Operations

  • Archive: prevents new events, preserves history
  • Delete: permanently removes record, events, and container. Running sessions cannot be deleted (must send interrupt first).

Agent Configuration

FieldDescription
nameRequired. Human-readable name
modelRequired. Claude model (all 4.5+ models supported)
systemSystem prompt for behavior and persona
toolsPre-built agent tools, MCP tools, and custom tools
mcp_serversMCP servers for standardized third-party capabilities
skillsSkills for domain-specific context with progressive disclosure
callable_agentsOther agents this agent can invoke (multi-agent, research preview)
descriptionDescription of what the agent does
metadataArbitrary key-value pairs for tracking

Update Semantics

  • Omitted fields are preserved
  • Scalar fields (model, system, name) are replaced
  • Array fields (tools, mcp_servers, skills, callable_agents) are fully replaced
  • Metadata is merged at key level; set value to empty string to delete a key
  • Updates generate a new agent version; no-op updates do not create new versions

Agent Lifecycle

  • Update: generates a new agent version
  • Archive: agent becomes read-only; new sessions cannot reference it; existing sessions continue
  • Versions start at 1 and increment on each update

Multi-Agent: callable_agents (Research Preview)

Managed Agents support multi-agent orchestration via the callable_agents field. Key architecture details:

  • All agents share the same container and filesystem
  • Each agent runs in its own session thread (context-isolated event stream with own conversation history)
  • Only one level of delegation: coordinator can call agents, but those agents CANNOT call agents of their own
  • Tools and context are NOT shared between agents

Session Threads

  • Session-level stream = primary thread (condensed view of all activity)
  • Individual thread streams for per-agent reasoning and tool calls
  • If any thread is running, session is running
  • List threads: GET /v1/sessions/:id/threads
  • Stream per-thread: GET /v1/sessions/:id/threads/:thread_id/stream

Multi-Agent Event Types

TypeDescription
session.thread_createdCoordinator spawned a new thread
session.thread_idleAn agent thread finished its current work
agent.thread_message_sentAgent sent message to another thread
agent.thread_message_receivedAgent received message from another thread

CLI: ant

The ant CLI is the command-line tool for working with Managed Agents. Installation methods:

  • Homebrew (macOS): brew install anthropics/tap/ant
  • Linux/WSL: download release binary from GitHub
  • Go: go install github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cli/cmd/ant@latest

Custom Tools

Custom tools in Managed Agents work like user-defined client tools in the Messages API. The model emits a structured request; YOUR code runs the operation; the result flows back. Best practices include extremely detailed descriptions (3-4+ sentences), consolidating related operations with an action parameter, meaningful namespacing in tool names, and returning only high-signal information.

SDK Support and Vault Authentication

SDKs are available for Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, C#, Ruby, and PHP. Pass vault_ids at session creation for MCP tools requiring authentication; Anthropic manages token refresh on your behalf.

Key Exam Facts
  • Beta header: managed-agents-2026-04-01 (SDK sets automatically)
  • 4 core concepts: Agent, Environment, Session, Events
  • Agent toolset type: agent_toolset_20260401 with 8 built-in tools
  • Session starts idle; must send user.message to begin work
  • Session statuses: idle, running, rescheduling, terminated
  • Rate limits: 60/min create, 600/min read
  • Multi-agent: one level of delegation only; threads share filesystem but NOT context
  • Archiving an agent = read-only; existing sessions continue
  • Deleting a session = permanent; running sessions cannot be deleted
  • All Claude 4.5+ models supported
  • Vault IDs enable MCP authentication at session creation
summary: Managed Agents provide a fully managed agent harness running in Anthropic's infrastructure, built around four pillars: Agent, Environment, Session, and Events. The agent_toolset_20260401 provides 8 built-in tools (bash, read, write, edit, glob, grep, web_fetch, web_search). Sessions start idle and require a user.message event to begin. Multi-agent orchestration via callable_agents supports one level of delegation with context-isolated threads sharing a filesystem. Beta header: managed-agents-2026-04-01. Rate limits: 60/min create, 600/min read.

key_concepts.definition: Agent (model + prompt + tools), Environment (container template), Session (running instance), Events (messages between app and agent). The foundational architecture of Managed Agents.
The built-in toolset providing 8 tools: bash, read, write, edit, glob, grep, web_fetch, web_search. Can selectively enable/disable individual tools via configs array.
Sessions start idle, transition to running when a user.message event is sent. Statuses: idle, running, rescheduling, terminated. Creating a session provisions but does NOT start work.
Field on agent config enabling multi-agent orchestration. Only one level of delegation allowed. Agents share filesystem but have context-isolated threads.
In multi-agent sessions, each agent runs in its own thread with independent conversation history. The primary thread provides a condensed view of all activity.
managed-agents-2026-04-01 required for all requests. SDK sets it automatically. Enabled by default for all API accounts.
Agent versions are immutable. Updates generate a new version (starting at 1). Archiving makes the agent read-only but existing sessions continue.
Command-line tool for Managed Agents. Available via Homebrew, GitHub releases, or Go install.
